title: The World Baseball Classic is good because the best players care | CNN
text:
Source: CNN

Paul Skenes – pitching in what was undeniably the biggest game of his stunning-but-underutilized career so far and in what will certainly be the loudest stadium he experiences this year – hit Julio Rodríguez in the wrist with a 98 mile-per-hour fastball.

The extremely painful free pass loaded the bases for Team Dominican Republic in the bottom of the fourth inning afterTeam USA had just taken a 2-1 leadin the previous frame. It also probably caused a whole bunch of sports fans in the Pacific Northwest to temporarily curse the very existence of the World Baseball Classic. Just ahead of a season with so much promise for the Seattle Mariners,why was their young starrisking his health for the sake of a showcase?

About 10 minutes later, Rodríguez gave them the answer. Tracking a deep drive, he reached out that same hand to brace himself, scaling the outfield wall to steal a home run from the greatest power hitter on the planet. He made a fist, pumped his arms in an X-shape across his chest and roared – not in pain, but in power and pride. What separates a showcase from something with true stakes if not the emotional investment of the people involved?

Later, Aaron Judge, the man robbed of the home run but also the author of an incredible defensive play of his own, declared the WBC crowds“bigger and better than the World Series.”

It’s not the only time this tournament thatplayers have consideredwhich is the true pinnacle of the sport — the WBC or the World Series. Rodríguez, for instance, offered one of the more unequivocal answers: WBC. It’s a question that generates intriguing content but, for better or for worse, players don’t get to choose and fans don’t have to.

Sunday night’s semifinal matchup between Team USA and Team DR may have been the best contemporary baseball talent ever to assemble in one showdown. Actual World Series rosters include nine-hole hitters, bullpen bulk guys, and fifth starters.Even most recent All-Star Gamesaren’t as stacked by some measures. And the WBC has something every All-Star game across sports has struggled to legislate into existence: Effort, all funneled into a single-elimination format.

The WBC has found the formula: All-Star lineups thrust into Game 7-style stakes.

The World Baseball Classic has gotit. Google trends indicate interest was higher in the US vs. Dominican game than in the reveal of the NCAA Tournament bracket on Selection Sunday. Baseball may be the center of the sports world in late October; but it’s alsocaptivating audiencesnowin mid-March. The secret sauce is deceptively simple: great players giving it their all.

Luis Severino, starting for the DR one more time before he returns to an Athletics team projected to finish below .500,threw more pitches faster than 99 mphin 3.1 innings than he did all last season. He was guaranteed a short stint by the tournament rules, but certainly the stage and the circumstances and the decibel levels factored into his adrenaline as well.

Judge, who was speaking from a position of ample personal experience to compare the WBC to postseason baseball, gunned down Fernando Tatís Jr. at third with the hardest outfield throw he’s made in three years.

The investment manifests differently depending on the rosters. The Dominican team set a WBC record for home runs in this tournament – and, presumably, a record for ensuing bat flips and elaborate home run trots that turn a perfunctory trip around the bases into a performative celebration of national identity and personal swagger.

Team USA, by contrast, has approached gameslike a cross between a business meeting and a military operation. So serious about winning the Americans forgot it’s supposed to be fun. So committed to the contrivance of fighting for the name on the front of the jersey they’ve made an international event seem jingoistic. So explicit about honoring the troops – Robert O’Neill, the man who claims to have killed Osama bin Laden, was brought in to give a speech to the team last week – that I’m starting to wonder if they know what they do. Or what the troops do.

It’s an aggressive, no-nonsense, competitive energy. It’s offputting to some fans. But it’s also undeniably indicative of real buy-in.

Broadly speaking, other countries have always cared about the WBC – it’s not hard to understand why players who had to leave their homes to ply their trade relish the opportunity to represent their roots – and the evolving success of the tournament is best reflected in how seriously the US team takes it all. There’s some prize money involved, but it seems that what actually worked to motivate more investment was the competitive level set by countries like Japan and the DR.

All sporting events are showcases. The season structures and ranking systems – not to mention everything else about the rules – are completely made up. We just want to know who is the greatest and we try to approximate a fair enough answer by pitting greats against each other.

This year, the WBC believably does that. If you were at the game Sunday between Team USA and Team DR, it’s possible you’ll some day be able to say you saw 10 Hall of Famers on the same field. And they allreallywanted to win.

It’s what makes the ultimate ending – an obvious blown call for the final out – so unsatisfying. It’s why Juan Soto of the newly eliminated DR Team, which was averaging over 10 runs per game before they ran into Paul Skenes, canstill claim the tournament revealed the Dominicans to be the bestin baseball.

It’s why we’ll watch on Monday night when Italy and Venezuela play for the right to take on the USA in Tuesday’s final. And it’s why we’ll all be back in three years to watch what will surely be a similarly star-studded clash.
